content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 21, 1990

Robert C. Fletcher
Sportfishing Association of California
2917 Canon Street
San Diego, CA 92106

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-703

Dear Mr. Fletcher:

You have requested advice on behalf of the Sportfishing
Association of California concerning the campaign provisions of
the Political Reform Act.¹ In our telephone conversation of
November 27, 1990, you clarified that you were requesting formal
advice pursuant to Regulation 18329 (copy enclosed) rather than a
formal opinion.

QUESTION

Is the Sportfishing Association of California (the
"Association") required to register and file disclosure reports as
a recipient committee?

CONCLUSION

An organization which makes contributions from membership
dues it receives is deemed to be a recipient committee when it
makes contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year
from those funds, and its members have reason to know that a
portion of their dues payments may be used for making campaign
contributions or expenditures. The organization's members have
reason to know that a portion of their dues payments may be used
for political purposes if they have been so advised by the
organization, or if the organization has in the past made campaign
contributions or expenditures out of those funds.

___________________
¹ Government Code Sections §1000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Robert C. Fletcher
Page Two


FACTS

The Sportfishing Association of California was formed in 1972
to promote the interests of the southern California sportfishing
industry. The Association's operating budget comes from members'
dues, no portion of which is designated specifically for making
political contributions.

The Association has made political contributions to
California candidates and committees in previous years. Your
letter is unclear as to when the Association began making
contributions or what year the Association first made
contributions totaling $1,000 or more.

In 1990 you filed a Form 410 on behalf of the Association and
have received notice that your filing was late.

ANALYSIS

Section 82013 establishes the definition of a recipient
committee, which must file a statement of organization and
periodic campaign statements. (Sections 84101, 84200-84204.)
I explained to you in our telephone conversation of November 27,
1990, with respect to an organization which receives funds from
its members, but is formed primarily for purposes other than
making contributions to or expenditures on behalf of California
state and local candidates, ballot measures, or committees, the
organization qualifies as a recipient committee in the calendar
year in which it makes contributions or expenditures totaling
$1,000 or more and the members "know or have reason to know" that
some or all of their funds will be used to make contributions or
expenditures. The members of the Association had "reason to know"
once the Association had established a "history" for making such
expenditures, i.e., it had previously made contributions or
expenditures. (Regulation 18215(c), copy enclosed.)

Enclosed is a fact sheet entitled Organizations Which Receive
Funds for Purposes Other Than Making Contributions to or
Expenditures on Behalf of California State and Local Candidates,
Ballot Measures or Committees. This fact sheet will assist you in
your reporting obligations.

A statement of organization must be filed within 10 days of
qualifying as a recipient committee. (Section 84101.)

Your letter seems to indicate that the Association made
contributions in years preceding March 1989 when you became
president and treasurer. You may need to review the Association's
financial records to determine the actual date the Association
qualified as a recipient committee.

Robert C. Fletcher
Page Three


Please review the enclosed document for assistance in
reporting your contributions.

If you have additional questions, please contact me at (916)
322-5662.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: [illegible] Kevin S. Braaten-Moen
Political Reform Consultant

Enclosures

Robert C. Fletcher

Section 82013 establishes the definition of a recipient committee, which must file a statement of organization and periodic campaign statements. (Sections 84101, 84200-84204.) As I explained to you in our telephone conversation of November 27, 1990, with respect to an organization which receives funds from its members, but is formed primarily for purposes other than making contributions to or expenditures on behalf of California state and local candidates, ballot measures, or committees, the organization qualifies as a recipient committee in the calendar year in which it makes contributions or expenditures totaling $1,000 or more and the members "know or have reason to know" that some or all of their funds will be used to make contributions or expenditures. (Regulation 18215(c), copy enclosed.)

Enclosed is a fact sheet entitled Organizations Which Receive Funds for Purposes Other Than Making Contributions to or Expenditures on Behalf of California State and Local Candidates, Ballot Measures or Committees. This fact sheet will assist you in your reporting obligations.

CONCLUSION: An organization which makes contributions from membership dues it receives is deemed to be a recipient committee when it makes contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year from those funds, and its members have reason to know that a portion of their dues payments may be used for making campaign contributions or expenditures. The organization's members have reason to know that a portion of their dues payments may be used for political purposes if they have been so advised by the organization, or if the organization has in the past made campaign contributions or expenditures out of those funds.

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

Robert C. Fletcher
